replies.author: @MrModel--CAPTURED-ON-FILM
replies.text: @TheSuperappelflap What is a little funny about all of this, to me, is, back in the 90s I imagined all of this happening one day in the not too distant future. That new films might be made with a computer contrived Marilyn Monroe or Humphrey Bogart. My friends thought I was crazy. But I was right (insert: "I told you so" here). When digital cameras could only do as much as 2 pix, I bought a very expensive Pentax film camera, not believing film would ever be replaced.  That Pentax has sat in my drawer, unused, for years.  These is no place I can have 35mm 'film' developed and printed anywhere these days.  AI is going to replace a lot, and probably make something new we've never seen before.  Most alarming to me is how it will re-write the past, our history, and future gens will not know what is true. Right now it's a teenage guitar player in his parent's garage with a cheap amp.  And even the family dog is hiding under a pillow to escape the noise.  Someday AI will fill stadiums and control the media.  If you and I are still communicating I just might be sending you "I told you so".  I would humbly suggest learning AI, discovering something you can use it for to the left or to the right, something different than how everyone else is using it.  Something unique, that only you can do.

replies.author: @nunyabusiness9433
replies.text:
I mean, it was sort of inevitable.  Netflix was going gangbusters with virtually zero competition, and at least one of the services, HBO/Max, was a high grade major production studio in its own right who always hosted their own content even on cable all the way back to the 90's.  It was mergers that led to Hulu being the dumping ground of all the non-Disney content they suddenly ended up with, and Disney had enough back catalog I was watching endless cartoons back in the days when you could pull it free over the air with analog dishes; pretty much a no brainer for them, then.  Paramount and Peacock are sort of also-rans.  Yeah, they make stuff, but outside of a couple of flagship IP's, they weren't major players.

I think it was just a matter of timing.  Had Covid not happened, things would have shaken out like the did, just more slowly.  Covid just accelerated everything and put a magnifying glass to every nuance and poured barrels of gasoline (or dollars, actually) into "that streaming thing".

author: @CriticalThinker-i6e
text: Hollywood is collapsing under the weight of its own hypocrisy. For the most part, the industry is filled with people who are either outright predatory or too weak and complicit to call any of it out. Acting, by its nature, attracts people who are comfortable being disingenuous, and the few decent individuals in the industry keep themselves far from the circus. But the rest? They’ve stood by while the sexual abuse, the predatory behaviour, and the ideological weaponisation of entertainment have taken over. For over 15 years, Hollywood has traded genuine storytelling for propaganda, using their platform not to inspire or entertain but to push agendas. They were supposed to celebrate human stories — fictional or historic — and instead they’ve turned themselves into a factory of manipulation. And when people ask, “Why is Hollywood dying?” the answer is brutally simple: because they killed it themselves.

author: @GartasABV
text: 2:41  I studied business, so my angle comes from that background. When a company whose core engine is creativity loses that creativity, it starts producing bad films and naturally begins losing money, as we see in Hollywood. The industry is likely constrained by a culture that blocks open communication. Considering what we’ve seen in recent years—companies hiring people based solely on skin color, ethnicity, or race, without the required qualifications—that likely contributed to the decline of creativity and the disappearance of innovative projects. With Hollywood performing poorly, they also avoid taking risks on new ideas. One thing I’ve learned is that it’s never a single mistake that causes a collapse; it’s the accumulation of multiple errors. Poor sales, “woke” hiring practices that placed unqualified people in key roles, possible foreign pressure as anime gains more global influence, and the push of political agendas—together these forces likely damaged creativity. This isn’t only visible in Hollywood but also in the video game industry. I enjoy video games, and I see the same decline: American companies that were once excellent no longer innovate, release weak products, and then wonder how a project that took seven years to develop failed. There is clearly a severe problem in the United States rooted in organizational culture.

author: @ShaneCurtis-ru1zc
text:
It really is so sad as far as what HOLLYWOOD used to be. I'm 41 right now, born in '84 and even I remember being a kid - probably around 10 or 11 - and when the Oscars were on TV, that was a huge deal. And while Hollywood has always been a business...all the major studios competing for contracts with the biggest A-listers...it used to rely so much on the art. The art of the filmmaking...the writing, cinematography, directing, editing.... getting amazing actors and actresses front and center to bring this magic to life, where going to see these wonderful motion pictures in a dark theatre, surrounded by other movie goers was nothing short of an amazing experience. 
   I remember my Grandma talking about old Hollywood...all the rumors and secrets surrounding these beautiful people...Humphrey Bogart, Bette Davis, Judy Garland, Joan Crawford, Clark Gable...and so on. And even in the early 90s, when we still had such a cherished group of adult performers putting out films...Jack Nicholson, Shirley MaClaine, Jessica Lange, John Candy, Kathy Bates, Patrick Swayze, Robin Williams, and so so many more. 
   But nowadays?? Yeah we still have some of those great actors, but their hands are tied too...or they go right along with the woke garbage that destroying their own industry (along with many other industries)...now all that's offered is weak-ass stories that have been done so many times... except now they're gonna "remake it better...ya know, with CGI." Can't even have a CHARACTER anymore because we have to be politically correct and not offend ANYONE....So Ariel will be black...with all her sisters each being a DIFFERENT nationality or race. Instead of putting films in theatres, people can just STREAM it on their phone while taking a shit in the morning. It just sucks now. And we wonder why "Hollywood" has gone to hell. There's no respect or independent thought anymore, that's why. Nobody can really be creative or strive for what they really want to create, because then some movie studio executive in a tie is gonna come by and tell you that certain scenes need cut due to schedule and costs, and you gotta do this and that...and by time it's all said and done, all that remains is a fraction of what you as a filmmaker wanted. The writer gets screwed. The director gets screwed. And all the loan sharks care about are opening weekend box office numbers....a profit. Yet if they let real creativity happen, that's when you get memorable and great movies. The 80s?? There was CONSTANTLY hits! Every week! That are known by people all over today! TERMINATOR, DIRTY DANCING, E.T., THE LOST BOYS, AN OFFICER AND A GENTLEMAN, TOP GUN, CHRISTINE, GREMLINS, THE GOONIES, CREEPSHOW, CHEECH AND CHONG'S "UP IN SMOKE," GREASE, TERMS OF ENDEARMENT...and the list goes on and on and on. I'm afraid those days are long gone. I feel so lucky to have witnessed it when it was really something to experience and truly could transport you to another world...and celebrities were all about their craft and not preaching politics at you, telling you how you should feel abd vote.
